mission.title: Install and Configure KubeFlex (Multi-Tenant Control Planes)
mission.description: KubeFlex is a KubeStellar project that provides dedicated Kubernetes control planes per tenant. It enables scalable multi-tenant control plane management where each tenant gets their own API server, scheduler, and controller manager — giving a 'vanilla K8s experience' without cluster sprawl.

steps.title: Install KubeFlex using Helm
steps.description:
Add the KubeStellar Helm repository and install KubeFlex:
```bash
helm repo add kubestellar https://kubestellar.github.io/helm
helm repo update
helm install kubeflex kubestellar/kubeflex --namespace kubeflex-system --create-namespace
```
> This installs the KubeFlex controller and CRDs.

steps.title: Verify KubeFlex controller is running
steps.description:
Check that the KubeFlex controller pod is running:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kubeflex-system
kubectl get crds | grep kubeflex
```
> You should see the kubeflex-controller pod in Running state and ControlPlane CRD registered.

steps.title: Create a tenant control plane
steps.description:
Create a dedicated control plane for a tenant:
```bash
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: tenancy.kubeflex.io/v1alpha1
kind: ControlPlane
metadata:
  name: tenant-1
spec:
  type: k8s
  backend: shared
EOF
```
> This creates a virtual control plane named 'tenant-1'.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: tenancy.kubeflex.io/v1alpha1
kind: ControlPlane
metadata:
  name: tenant-1
spec:
  type: k8s
  backend: shared
EOF

steps.title: Verify the control plane
steps.description:
Check that the control plane is provisioned and ready:
```bash
kubectl get controlplanes
kubectl get pods -l kubeflex.io/control-plane=tenant-1
```
> The control plane should show as 'Ready' and its pods should be running.

steps.title: Access the tenant control plane
steps.description:
Get the kubeconfig for the tenant control plane:
```bash
kubectl get secret tenant-1-admin-kubeconfig -o jsonpath='{.data.kubeconfig}' | base64 -d > tenant-1-kubeconfig.yaml
KUBECONFIG=tenant-1-kubeconfig.yaml kubectl get nodes
```
> This extracts the kubeconfig for direct access to the tenant's API server.
commands: kubectl get secret tenant-1-admin-kubeconfig -o jsonpath='{.data.kubeconfig}' | base64 -d > tenant-1-kubeconfig.yaml

uninstall.title: Delete all control planes
uninstall.description:
Remove all tenant control planes first:
```bash
kubectl delete controlplanes --all
```
> Wait for all control plane pods to terminate.

uninstall.title: Uninstall KubeFlex
uninstall.description:
Remove the KubeFlex Helm release:
```bash
helm uninstall kubeflex -n kubeflex-system
kubectl delete namespace kubeflex-system
kubectl delete crd controlplanes.tenancy.kubeflex.io
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade KubeFlex
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm chart and upgrade:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kubeflex kubestellar/kubeflex -n kubeflex-system
kubectl get pods -n kubeflex-system
```
> Existing control planes will continue running during the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Control plane stuck in Provisioning
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Insufficient resources or RBAC issues preventing API server creation.

**Fix:** Check the KubeFlex controller logs:
```bash
kubectl logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=kubeflex -n kubeflex-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Cannot access tenant API server
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The control plane service may not be exposed or the kubeconfig secret is not yet created.

**Fix:** Wait for the control plane to reach Ready state, then retry:
```bash
kubectl get controlplanes -w
```

resolution.summary: You should see the KubeFlex controller running in kubeflex-system namespace and be able to create ControlPlane resources that provision dedicated API servers per tenant.

prerequisites.resources: Minimum 2 vCPU, 2GB RAM for the controller + 1 vCPU, 1GB per control plane
prerequisites.description: A running Kubernetes cluster with Helm 3 installed.
